context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on progress, modernization, development, and planning. i’m kinda stuck on how to separate those terms + connect western vs non-western ideas of progress.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig in. Before we tackle the distinctions between those terms, tell me: in your own words, what does 'progress' mean to you? Don't overthink it — just your gut sense of the word.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and needs orientation rather than content delivery. I should use the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge before diving into the terms. I'll ask a simple opening question to get them thinking about what 'progress' means to them, which will naturally lead into the bigger conceptual distinctions the prompt asks for. Minimal scaffolding at this stage.

exchanges.student: my gut says progress = things getting “better” over time, but idk better for who lol. like more wealth/tech/rights maybe?
exchanges.tutor: That 'better for who' instinct is actually the sharpest part of your answer — hold onto it. Now think about this: if different societies might define 'better' differently, what does that tell you about whether progress is a *universal* concept or something more specific to a particular culture or time period?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid intuitive answer and already flagged a key critical insight ('better for who') without realizing it. I should affirm that instinct and push them to develop it further, since that tension is at the heart of the prompt's question about whether progress is universal or culturally specific. I'll ask a focused Socratic question to move them toward the Western/non-Western dimension.

exchanges.student: i think maybe both? like the idea of improvement feels kinda universal, but the actual content of “better” seems super culture/time specific.
exchanges.tutor: Good distinction. So if the *content* of 'better' is culture-specific, do you think the Western Enlightenment invented the idea of progress, or were there earlier or non-Western societies that also had their own visions of improvement and a better future?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good distinction between the abstract idea of improvement (potentially universal) and its specific content (culturally contingent). This maps well onto the prompt's question about whether progress is universal. I should push them to now connect this to the historical/geographic origin question — specifically the Enlightenment and non-Western formations — since that's the next key part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah i don’t think the Enlightenment fully invented it, just maybe turned it into a big historical theory. i’m guessing earlier empires/religions had ideas of moral or social improvement too, but i’m fuzzy on examples.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Think about what you know about ancient civilizations or major world religions — do any of them have built-in ideas about human improvement, moral development, or a better society? Even one example would strengthen your argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and has arrived at a solid hypothesis. Rather than giving them examples directly, I should prompt them to think harder about what they already know. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. I'll nudge them toward specific domains they might draw from — without naming the examples for them.

exchanges.student: maybe Confucian thought? like better society through moral cultivation + good rule, not “progress” as nonstop innovation tho. i’m less sure abt other examples.
exchanges.tutor: Confucianism is a great example, and your nuance about it *not* being about endless innovation is actually important — it hints at different *models* of progress. Now let's move to the next piece of the prompt: how would you distinguish 'progress' from 'modernization'? What feels different about those two words to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has come up with a solid non-Western example (Confucianism) and made an important nuance — that non-Western progress ideas aren't necessarily about linear innovation. This is a key insight for the paper. I should affirm this and push them to now move toward the next part of the prompt: distinguishing between progress, modernization, development, and planning. We've spent enough time on the origin question and need to move forward.

exchanges.student: umm modernization feels more specific/material? like industrialization, bureaucracy, science, nation-state stuff. progress feels broader + more value-loaded.
exchanges.tutor: Good — so if modernization is more concrete and institutional, where does 'development' fit in? How is it similar to or different from modernization?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a good distinction. I should affirm it and push them to now extend the chain to 'development' and 'planning,' which are the remaining terms in the prompt. We need to keep moving forward efficiently given the planned conversation length.

exchanges.student: i’m stuck rn tbh. maybe development is modernization but with economics/policy added? not sure if that’s too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Not too simplistic at all. Think about *when* the word 'development' became widely used in international policy — roughly post-WWII, decolonization era. Who was using it, and about whom? That context might help you sharpen the distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck but their instinct is reasonable. Rather than explaining development, I should ask a targeted question to help them think it through. I'll point them toward the context in which 'development' emerged as a term — post-WWII, decolonization — without giving it away.

exchanges.student: ok wait, i think development is more of a postwar global policy framework, where states + intl orgs label some countries “underdeveloped” and try to move them toward growth. so it overlaps w modernization but feels more hierarchical/political.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — 'hierarchical/political' is key. So where does *planning* fit into this chain? What role does it play in relation to development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Excellent insight — the student has independently arrived at the hierarchical/political dimension of development, which is central to the course themes. Now I should push them to complete the chain by defining 'planning' and its relationship to the other terms. We're also getting close to the point where I should ask them to start integrating these ideas into written content.

exchanges.student: i think planning is the tool? like the state or donors use planning to turn ideas of development into actual policies, projects, and institutions.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you now have a working distinction for all four terms. Before we go further, I'd suggest you start drafting. You've built up solid ideas here, but there are still parts of the prompt you haven't touched yet — like the relationship between *tradition* and *modernity* in development. Try writing a first paragraph or two integrating what we've discussed, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all four key terms reasonably well. We've covered the main conceptual ground across several exchanges. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, it's time to ask the student to start synthesizing what we've discussed into actual written content for their reflection piece. I should also remind them that the prompt still has parts they haven't addressed — specifically the relationship between tradition and modernity, and how different planning traditions draw on different aspects of modernity.

exchanges.student: yeah that helps, but i still need help w/ the tradition vs modernity part. i keep wanting to say tradition blocks development, but that feels too blunt/wrong.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question that. Think about it this way: who historically *defined* tradition as an obstacle to development, and what might their motives have been in framing it that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is sensing that the 'tradition blocks development' framing is wrong but can't articulate why. This is a productive moment of cognitive dissonance. Rather than explaining the tradition/modernity relationship, I should ask a targeted Socratic question to help them question their own assumption. This connects directly to the course theme about colonialism and institutional legacies.
